1: koh her'ani Adonai YHWH — Fourth vision: a basket of summer fruit (kelub qayits). Qayits (summer fruit, often figs at the end of the season) sets up one of Amos's most striking wordplays. The vision is presented and interpreted in immediate sequence — no intercession, no divine relenting.
2: ba ha-qetz el-ami Yisrael — "The end (qetz) has come upon my people Israel." The pun: qayits (summer fruit) sounds like qetz (end, termination). Summer fruit signals the end of the growing season — the last harvest before winter. The end the fruit announces is Israel's own termination. "I will never again pass over them" repeats the verdict of Amos 7:8 — the pattern of relenting is definitively closed.
3: ve-heylu shirot heikhal ba-yom hahu — "the songs of the temple shall become wailing (yeyalilu) in that day." The liturgical songs that fill the sanctuary will become death-wail. The many corpses thrown everywhere and the command "silence!" (has) repeats the terror-silence of Amos 6:10 — the name of YHWH too dangerous to invoke, the dead too many to count.
4: shim'u zot ha-sho'afim evyon — "Hear this, you who trample the needy." The indictment pivot. Sha'af (trample, pant after, crush) — the merchant class panting with desire for the poor to be economically consumed. They wait for the new moon to end and the sabbath to pass so commerce can resume — the sacred calendar is an obstacle to profit, not a structure for life.
5: lehaqtin eifah u-le-hagdil shekel — "making the ephah small and the shekel great." The eifah is the dry measure for grain — shrinking it means the customer gets less. The shekel is the weight for silver — inflating it means the price paid is higher. Both instruments of trade are falsified simultaneously. The moznei mirma (deceitful scales) complete the picture: every mechanism of exchange is corrupted from within.
6: likhnot ba-kesef dalim — "buying the poor for silver." The accumulation of economic injustice culminates in debt slavery. The needy is sold for a pair of sandals — the same image as Amos 2:6, forming an inclusio around the book's first major indictment section. Even the waste of the wheat (mappal bar — husks, chaff, or spoiled grain) is sold to the poor: they receive refuse at inflated prices.
7: nishba YHWH b'ge'on Yaakov — "YHWH swears by the pride of Jacob." In 6:8 the pride of Jacob is the object of divine abhorrence; here it is the instrument of the divine oath. The use of ge'on Yaakov as a swearing-formula may be ironic — YHWH swears by what Israel is most proud of precisely because that pride is what is being judged. The oath guarantees: "I will never forget any of their deeds."
8: ha-al zot lo tirgaz ha-aretz — "shall not the land tremble on account of this?" The cosmic response to injustice is seismic: the earth shaking, rising and sinking like the Nile in flood. The Nile comparison (ka-ye'or Mitzrayim) invokes the Egyptian context — the same land from which YHWH redeemed Israel becomes the image for Israel's judgment-upheaval.
9: ve-haya ba-yom hahu — "it shall be on that day." The Day of the LORD imagery intensifies: hashshi ha-shemesh ba-tzaharayim — "I will make the sun go down at noon." Solar darkening at midday inverts the normal order of daylight. "I will darken the earth on a bright day" — the language echoes the Egyptian plague of darkness and anticipates eschatological Day of the LORD imagery in the prophets and NT.
10: ve-hafakhti chageikhem l'evel — "I will turn your feasts into mourning." The covenant feasts (chagim) are inverted to grief. The mourning becomes like that for an yachid (only son) — the most acute grief Israel knew. The "bitterest of days" (ke-yom marah) closes the chiasm: what was festive becomes funereal, what was sacred becomes sorrowful.
11: hineh yamim ba'im — "behold, days are coming." The eschatological formula signals the culminating punishment: a famine of the word of YHWH. Not bread or water but davar YHWH — the oracular, authoritative, covenantal speech of God. Having rejected the word Amos proclaimed, Israel will search for it everywhere and find nothing. The withdrawal of prophetic speech is itself the judgment.
12: ve-nau mi-yam ad yam — "they shall wander from sea to sea." The geography of desperation: from west to east, from north to south (Dan to Beersheba in reverse), seeking the word of YHWH. The verb nua (wander, totter, stagger) suggests aimless, unmoored movement — the covenant community without prophetic guidance has lost its organizing center.
13: ba-yom hahu titalafna ha-betulot ha-yafot — "in that day the beautiful young women (betulot) and the young men shall faint." The thirst described is for the word of YHWH (Amos 8:11) — spiritual desolation expressed as physical exhaustion. Those in the prime of life, who should have the most vitality, are the ones who collapse. The thirst for divine speech that will not come is more enfeebling than physical thirst.
14: ha-nishba'im b'ashmat Shomron — "those who swear by the shame of Samaria." Ashmat Shomron may be a deliberate deformation of the divine name or a reference to a specific pagan deity worshipped there (possibly Ashimah — cf. 2 Kings 17:30). Swearing "by the way of Beersheba" suggests pilgrimage to a southern sanctuary as a form of religiosity divorced from covenant fidelity. These devotees of false shrines "shall fall and not rise again" — a fall with no restoration available.
